coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of tunable fluorescent carbon nanodots from Malus floribunda for sensors and multicolor bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Muthuchamy, Nallal
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: In this work, green and economical synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) is reported using Malus floribunda (M. floribunda) fruit and aqueous ammonia as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively by a simple hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-CNDs show exceptional advantages including acceptable fluorescent quantum yield (18 %) and N-CNDs sizes are around 3.5 nm. The fluorescence of N-CNDs based nanosensor can be selectively and sensitively quenched by ferric ions in the aqueous medium. This phenomenon was used to develop a fluorescent method for facile detection of ferric ions within a linear range of 10–50 μM with a detection limit of 2.5 μM (S/N = 5) having linear regression value (R2) of 0.994. The synthesized N-CNDs incubated with human colon cancer (HCT-116) cells which did not stimulate cell death, indicating that the N-CNDs have good biocompatibility. Hence, the N-CNDs was used as a fluorescent probe for in vitro imaging of HCT-116.TheN-CNDswereuniformly internalized into the whole body of cells and displays the multicolor fluorescence imaging. To best of the authors' knowledge, this is the earliest report on the selective sensing of ferric ions and live-cell imaging using M. floribunda fruit-derived N-CNDs by fluorescent quenching mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Malus floribunda
Carbon nanodot
Tunable fluorescence
Metal ion sensing
Turn-off fluorescence
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the synthesized N-CNDs
The sensing ability of the synthesized N-CNDs towards metal ions in an aqueous medium
Viability and imaging of human colon cancer cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ) and the Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government ( 2017R1C1B5076345 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure and favorable formation mechanism of N-CNDs using M. floribunda fruit and aqueous ammonia.

(a) and (b)TEM images, (c) and (d) HRTEM images of the N-CNDs. Inset (a) size histogram of the N-CNDs with a curve fitted to the data using a Gaussian model. Inset (d) lattice distance histogram of the N-CNDs obtained from HRTEM.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) ATR-FTIR spectrum of the synthesized N-CNDs.
(a) XPS survey-scan spectrum of the synthesized N-CNDs. High-resolution C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and O 1s (d) XPS spectra of the synthesized N-CNDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence excitation (λex) and emission (λem) spectra, (c) excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra, and (d) excitation-dependent normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized N-CNDsaqueous solution.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CNDs at different irradiation time, inset (a) fluorescence photographs of N-CNDs aqueous solution (0 min and 100th min of irradiation) under the 365 nm UV lamp and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CNDs at different storage time, inset (b) fluorescence photographs of N-CNDs aqueous solution (1st, 90th and 150th days) under the 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Selectivity of the fluorescence response of N-CNDs in the presence of various metal ions (each metal ion concentration was 1000 μM) with an excitation at 340 nm and (b) selective affinity test graphs of N-CNDs in the presence of different metal ions (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CNDs without and with 1000 μM metal ions, respectively). (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CNDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ and (d) Plot of the fluorescence intensity versus the concentrations of Fe3+ ((F0 − F)/F0) with the polynomial fit (100–1000 μM); Inset (d) linearly fitted plot of (d). (e) Fluorescence spectra of N-CNDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (10–50 μM) and (f) corresponding linearly fitted plot of (e).

Fluorescence photographic images of the N-CNDs aqueous solution in the presence of different metal ions with the concentration of 1000 μM for each metal ion under (a) daylight and (b) UV lamp (365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence microscopy images of human colon cancer (HCT-116) cells in the presence and absence of N-CNDs/N-CNDs + Fe3+under the different excitation wavelengths (405, 488 and 555 nm). Scale bar indicates 50 μm. The concentration of the N-CNDs is 100 μg mL−1.
Comparison of the present work with the reported work for the sensing of ferric ions by the carbonaceous nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of tunable fluorescent carbon nanodots from Malus floribunda for sensors and multicolor bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Pusan National University, Busan 46241, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Pusan National University, Busan 46241, Republic of Korea Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, green and economical synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) is reported using Malus floribunda (M. floribunda) fruit and aqueous ammonia as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively by a simple hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-CNDs show exceptional advantages including acceptable fluorescent quantum yield (18 %) and N-CNDs sizes are around 3.5 nm. The fluorescence of N-CNDs based nanosensor can be selectively and sensitively quenched by ferric ions in the aqueous medium. This phenomenon was used to develop a fluorescent method for facile detection of ferric ions within a linear range of 10–50 μM with a detection limit of 2.5 μM (S/N = 5) having linear regression value (R2) of 0.994. The synthesized N-CNDs incubated with human colon cancer (HCT-116) cells which did not stimulate cell death, indicating that the N-CNDs have good biocompatibility. Hence, the N-CNDs was used as a fluorescent probe for in vitro imaging of HCT-116.TheN-CNDswereuniformly internalized into the whole body of cells and displays the multicolor fluorescence imaging. To best of the authors' knowledge, this is the earliest report on the selective sensing of ferric ions and live-cell imaging using M. floribunda fruit-derived N-CNDs by fluorescent quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials have drawn increasing attention owing to extraordinary advantages such as photophysical properties, chemical stability, and good biocompatibility. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are primarily included in the fluorescent carbonaceous nanomaterials family with a particle size of less than 10 nm. Among carbon nanomaterials, CNDs have drawn the most extensive notice, owing to their amazing optical properties with good quantum yields, water-solubility, excellent photostability, and biocompatibility. Thus CNDs were shown as promising candidates for numerous exciting applications, such as biolabelling, bioimaging, drug delivery, catalysis, fluorescent sensors and optoelectronic devices [1–10]. In general, CNDs has been synthesized using physical and chemical methods. Physical methods include arc discharge, laser ablation/passivation, and plasma treatment. Chemical methods include electrochemical synthesis, combustion, acidic oxidation, hydrothermal, pyrolysis, microwave and ultrasonic. Among these methods, the hydrothermal carbonization has more attention owing to advantages such as reaction conditions and simple equipment. CNDs may be prepared using different precursors such as artificial sources/compounds and natural biosources/biowastes. The artificial compounds consist of organic compounds [11–14]. These artificial precursors delivered a good quantum yield, however, these precursors might be limited to low solubility in water, toxicity to the biological system and expensive to produce. The above drawbacks can be overcome by green synthesis route using natural biosources, plants leaves [15,16], fruits (lemon, kiwi, banana, gooseberry, peach, pear, avocado and etc) [17–20], seeds [21,22], roots [23,24] and other biowastes [25–28]. In general, the functionalization of CNDs and doping with heteroatoms are a successful approach to improve their fluorescence properties and quantum yield. Especially, the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) via the hydrothermal method has increased because it is a one-step process without further oxidation and passivation, has mild reaction conditions, and requires inexpensive equipment [29]. These hydrothermally synthesized N-CNDs, commonly used for the effective fluorescent sensing of metal ions. Although, the optical properties of N-CNDs obtained throughthe hydrothermal method for sensors need to be enhanced. Consequently, our goal is to synthesize N-CNDs via the hydrothermal method with high quantum yield (QY) for analytical applications in particular for sensing metal ions. The sensing/detection of metal ions through fluorescent N-CNDs has become more important, as metal ions have harmful impacts on human health and environmental fitness. Ferric ion (Fe3+) is an essential metal ion in both biological systems and environmental processes. To the variety of regulatory proteins present in the biological systems, Fe3+ ions can coordinate. For Parkinson’s disease, the concentration of Fe3+ ions plays an important role, as well as unnecessary Fe3+ ions, resulting in cytotoxicity. Fe3+ is a significant pollutant present in the environment [30]. For these reasons, sensitive and selective sensors for the detection of Fe3+ concentrations are needed. Consequently, the fluorescent method is a powerful tool for the detection of ions since it is a simple and high sensitivity towards detection. Even though N-CNDs have become powerful tools for chemosensing and biosensing based on their tunable fluorescence properties, a universal explanation for the fluorescence origin of N-CNDs has not yet been proposed clearly.

In this present study, highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs)was synthesized using Malus floribunda (M. floribunda) fruit and aqueous ammonia as carbon and nitrogen source, respectively through a simple hydrothermal-assisted carbonization method with an acceptable QY (18 %) and without high temperature and pressure. Here, M. floribunda fruits and aqueous ammonia are easily available and they are cost-effective materials. One of the most noteworthy characteristics of synthesized N-CNDs is their tunable fluorescence properties. These N-CNDs used as a fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. Furthermore, we also described the biomedical applications of the N-CNDs such as live-cell imaging and intracellular monitoring of Fe3+. The results reveal that N-CNDs is a good candidate for sensing Fe3+ in the real biological systems without any interference of new metal ions. Thus, the synthesized N-CNDs can be used for analytical applications in chemosensing, biosensing, and bioimaging.

By a simple hydrothermal method, N-CNDs was synthesized utilizing theM. Floribunda fruit along with aqueous ammonia. M. floribunda fruit was cut into small pieces and ground well with DI water. Followed by the grounded mixture was passed through a filter paper (Whatman) and the juice was collected. 1 mL of aqueous ammonia was mixed with 50 mL of M. floribunda fruit juice. The mixture was taken in a Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Subsequently the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and then filtered through a pore size of 0.22 μm mixed cellulose esters membrane filter to eradicate the larger particles. The supernatant was collected which emits intense blue fluorescence below the UV light (365 nm) irradiation. A portion of the supernatant solution was freeze-dried and was used for further work.

The QY of the synthesized N-CNDs was calculated by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QYR is 0.54) as a standard reference and was calculated using the following Eq. (1) [7,8]:
(1)
QY = Q
Y
R
I S
A R
(
n S
)
2
I R
A S
(
n R
)
2
where, “I” is the measured integrated fluorescent emission intensity, “n” is the refractive index of the solvent, and “A” is the absorbance (intensity). The subscript “R” and “S” refers to the known fluorescent reference and standard for the synthesized sample, respectively.

At room temperature, the detection of Fe3+ was performed using normal quartz cuvette by exciting around 340 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra were measured using the mixture of 0.5 mL, N-CNDs and 0.5 mL, DI water. The selectivity of Fe3+ using prepared N-CNDs was measured by adding 0.5 mL of twelve different common metal ions solutions (Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+ and ions) with 1 mM concentration to 0.5 mL solution of N-CNDs. By adopting the same procedure as that of a blank sample at room temperature, after 1 min of reaction time, the emission spectra were recorded. Then, the sensitivity of Fe3+ ions in the presence of N-CNDs was conducted as follows; 0.5 mL of N-CNDs in the quartz cuvette, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (10–1000 μM, 0.5 mL) was added followed by the fluorescence intensity was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the synthesized N-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Highly fluorescent N-CNDs were synthesized using M. floribunda fruit and aqueous ammonia as a carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. Fig. 1 show the representative synthetic procedure and a plausible mechanism for the formation of N-CNDs. The detailed mechanism for the synthesis of N-CNDs from the M. floribunda fruit as follows: M. floribunda fruit contains phenols, flavonoids, tannins, anthocyanins and so on, which undergoes dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization during the hydrothermal process which resulting in the N-CNDs [31–33]. The functional groups possessing over the carbon surface according to the phytoconstituents of M. floribunda fruit that will be confirmed by various analytical techniques. The surface morphology and crystallinity of the synthesized N-CNDs were revealed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) analysis. Fig. 2 shows the TEM and HRTEM images of synthesized N-CNDs with different magnifications. TEM images in Fig. 2(a) and (b) describe the spherical shape of N-CNDs histogram of inset Fig. 2(a) shows the N-CNDs diameter as ca. 100 N-CNDs which are measured at different locations on the grid. N-CNDs exhibit a relatively narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 3.5 nm which obtained on the basis of fitting the histogram to a Gaussian model. Fig. 2(c) and (d) show the HRTEM images of N-CNDs, illustrating that the N-CNDs are crystalline/graphitic nature and form a single contiguous crystalline phase. The well-resolved lattice fringes are shown in Fig. 2(d), obtaining a lattice distance of ca. 0.21 nm corresponds to the d-spacing of (100) plane which matches well with the reported for graphitic carbon dots [34,35]. Furthermore, the lattice distance of N-CNDs was confirmed through the layer distance histogram by HRTEM is shown in inset Fig. 2(d). The obtained ten interplanar distance is around 2.078 nm (each interplanar distance is ∼0.21 nm) which matches well with the calculated interplanar distance.

The graphitization of the synthesized N-CNDs was revealed by XRD analysis (Fig. 3 (a)). The broad diffraction band is observed around 24.5° and small diffraction band is observed around 43.2° which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (002) and (100) planes, respectively [17,36]. These XRD patterns have a good matching with the characteristic peaks of graphene oxide/graphitic carbon materials. The lattice distance of the synthesized N-CNDs is ca. 0.36 and 0.21 nm corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes, respectively. The corresponding (002) plane interlayer distance of 0.36 nm is slightly larger than the interlayer distance of bulk graphite/typical graphene (0.34 nm). Thus, N-CNDs may have little turbostratic carbon structure predominantly with graphitic carbon structure [37]. In addition, the larger interplanar distance is owing to the existence of functional groups on the surface of N-CNDs [38]. The lattice/interplanar distance is ca. 0.21 nm which shows a similar result as obtained using HRTEM lattice/interplanar distance of N-CNDs (Fig. 2(d)). As a consequence the synthesized N-CNDs exhibit a high degree of graphitization/crystallinity. Furthermore, the degree of graphitization/crystallinity and carbon structure of the synthesized N-CNDs are confirmed by the Raman spectroscopy. Raman spectrum of N-CNDs (Fig. S1) displays two different signature peaks centered at 1361 corresponds to D-band and at 1586 cm−1 ascribed to the G-band [39,40]. The D-band accounts for defects and disordered crystalline carbon structure of the sp3 cluster associated with amorphous carbon. The G-band confirms the existence of the sp2 hybridized (crystalline/graphite) core [41,42]. In general, the intensity ratio ID/IG of the Raman is often used to determine the degree of graphitization of carbon structure. The intensity ratio of D to G-bands is 0.8, (intensity ratio of D to D-bands is 1.2) revealing the graphitic/crystalline carbon phase of N-CNDs [43,44]. This agrees well with the HRTEM and XRD analyses. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectroscopy of the synthesized N-CNDs confirms the existence of functional groups on the surface of the carbon structure. The attenuated total reflectance-FTIR (ATR-FTIR) spectrum of N-CNDs (Fig. 3(b)) shows the absorption bands around 3300 and 2924 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH and CH functional groups, respectively [45–48]. The OH group might be originated from surface functionalization and or from adsorbed water molecules. The absorption bands at 1700 cm−1 correspond to CO/CN, 1606 cm−1 ascribed to CC, 1438 cm−1 recognized as CN, 1370 and 1202 cm−1 are attributed to COH and COC stretching vibrations, respectively [46,49–51]. This results suggesting the existence of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups over the surface of the synthesized N-CNDs. In addition, the nitrogen atom chemically bonded with the carbon framework (CNC bond formation).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were employed to investigate the elemental composition and the surface states of the synthesized N-CNDs. The survey-scan spectrum of N-CNDs (Fig. 4 (a)) shows three distinct peaks located around 285, 400 and 532 eV correspond to C 1s of sp2 C, N 1s of the doped nitrogen, and O 1s of the oxygen functional groups, respectively. The N-CNDs contains 72, 20 and 8 atomic percentage of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen at the corresponding binding energies, respectively (Inset Fig. 4(a)). Deconvolution of high-resolved C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4(b)) showed five component peaks with binding energies at 284.2, 284.7, 285.8, 288.2 and 290.7 eV, which can be ascribed to CC, CC, COH/CH, CNC/COC and OCOH bonds, respectively [8,52,53]. In addition, the satellite peak appeared at the binding energies of 293.4 eV. The partial XPS spectra of N 1s (Fig. 4(c)) can be deconvoluted into three component peaks centered at 398.5, 400.0 and 402.2 eV, which can be ascribed to pyridinic CNC, pyrrolic NH and graphitic N(C)3 groups, respectively [54–56]. The high-resolved N 1s spectrum reveals the presence of nitrogen bonded in/on the carbon framework which is consensus with the FTIR results. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the high-resolved O 1s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks with binding energy level around 531.2, 532.0, 533.3 and 536.2 eV attributed to CO, COH/COC, OCOH, and HOH bonds, respectively [20,57–59]. Thus the synthesized N-CNDs have nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups, which is a concurrence results with the FTIR.

The optical properties of N-CNDs were measured using ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra and fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5 (a)) display the intense broadband around 285 nm and low-intensity shoulder band around 328 nm, which may be correlated to π–π* transition of the nanocarbon and n–π*transition of the functional group at the surface, respectively [38,60]. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra are displayed as Fig. 5(b), an excitation peak around 340 nm and an emission peak around 418 nm at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. In addition, the N-CNDs was also subjected to different excitation wavelengths from 270 to 500 nm to resolve the better optical properties (Figs. 5(c) and S2). While increasing the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually up to an optimum level then diminished. The maximum (bright) fluorescence was observed by the excitation wavelength of 340 nm with the Stokes shift of 78 nm. In addition, it is noticed that an increase in excitation, shift in peak to longer wavelengths is observed which is clear from the normalized spectra of excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 5(d)). The results strongly suggesting that the fluorescence emissions of the synthesized N-CNDs were excitation-dependent [61]. The N-CNDs emit a wide range of fluorescence with a QY of 18 % and also shows a blue fluorescence under the illumination of the UV lamp (365 nm). The origin of the optical properties of N-CNDs is still hotly debated and is yet not well understood. Therefore, mechanisms involving quantum confinement, surface traps, the formation of aromatic structures and recombination of excitons have been proposed [62]. On the basis of the remarkable optical properties of the synthesized N-CNDs, it is potentially useful in bioimaging, and sensing applications.

The photobleaching stability and long-term stability of the synthesized N-CNDs are important for real-time optical applications. For the determination of photobleaching stability, the N-CNDs aqueous solution was irradiated under the UV lamp (8 W) with an excitation of 365 nm for 100 min and the fluorescence intensity was measured with 10 min intervals. Fig. 6 (a) exemplifies the changes in the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs at different irradiation times (0–100 min). The original fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs retains even after 100 min of exposure with strong UV irradiation. In addition, inset Fig. 6(a) shows the photographs of N-CNDs aqueous solution before and after irradiation under the 365 nm UV lamp. From the inset, it is clear that the negligible changes in the fluorescence after irradiation suggest the stability of synthesized N-CNDs under UV lamp irradiation [63]. Further, the N-CNDs aqueous solution was stored for a long time and measured the fluorescence intensity at different storage times (1–150 days). No obvious aggregation or change in fluorescence intensity was observed even after storage for several months, which is attributed to the electrostatic repulsions between the N-CNDs Fig. 6(b) [64]. The photographs of fluorescence emission of N-CNDs aqueous solution under the 365 nm UV lamp at various storage time is shown in inset Fig. 6(b).This suggesting that the N-CNDs exhibits stable fluorescence for a long time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing ability of the synthesized N-CNDs towards metal ions in an aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized N-CNDs exhibit considerable photostability and long time storage stability with satisfactory quantum yield. In addition, the N-CNDs terminated with carboxyl/hydroxyl groups on the surface are successfully synthesized which is clear from the FTIR spectroscopy and XPS analysis. The presence of these groups imparted excellent solubility in water without any further chemical modification. Therefore, it was expected that the fluorescence properties and excellent solubility in water of N-CNDs change by the reaction of metal ions. To best of this author's awareness recently ferric ions (Fe3+) are the most extensively reported metal ion targeted through fluorescent carbon dots. In general, iron plays a vital part in our life, and iron deficiency needs attention among the global micronutrient deficiencies. The presence of ferric ions in biological and environment system has to be balanced and monitored [65]. Many researchers [60–67] applied the quenching phenomena of carbon dots upon the addition of ferric ions. The respective percentage resembles the concentrations of ferric ions. In this work, the selective detection of Fe3+ ion by N-CNDs probe was performed using a 1 mM concentration of different metal ions (Fig. 7 (a)). The synthesized N-CNDs aqueous solution emitted a strong fluorescence at a wavelength of 418 nm by the excitation wavelength of 340 nm with a Stokes shift of 78 nm which is denoted as blank. The fluorescence intensities of the N-CNDs was measured with the addition of different metal ions. Fig. 7(a) represents the changes in fluorescence intensities by the addition of metal ions. Comparison with blank, the fluorescence intensity is slightly enhanced by the addition of Al3+. Only Ferric ion is remarkably quenched (turn-off) the fluorescence intensity of the N-CNDs over the 12 metal ions, suggesting that the synthesized N-CNDs selectively detected the ferric ion. The quenching of fluorescence is associated with the strong coordination between Fe3+ ions and the hydroxyl/carboxyl or nitrogen functional groups of N-CNDs, which leads to a stable complex. The fluorescence quenching mechanism occurs by the partial electron transfer to the d orbital of Fe3+ in the excited state. The coordination (affinity) between different metal ions and N-CNDs is shown in Fig. 7(b). Among different metal ions, ferric ion showed a high affinity towards N-CNDs. Hence, the further investigation conducted about the fluorescence of N-CNDs in the presence of Fe3+ at different concentrations and the corresponding fluorescence spectra is shown as Fig. 7(c). The spectra reveal that the fluorescence intensities of the N-CNDs are sensitive to Fe3+ concentration. The fluorescence intensities decrease with an increase in Fe3+ concentration. The relationship between the concentration of Fe3+and fluorescence intensities ((F0 – F)/F0) of N-CNDs was fitted as shown in Fig. 7(d). A quick fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ ions is observed as a consequence good linear relationship for low concentrations and diverted curve for high concentrations is obvious from Fig. 7(d). Estimation of the limit of detection (LOD) was difficult, thus the quenching experiment was performed using low concentration from 10 to 50 μM of Fe3+ ion and corresponding fluorescence spectra depicted as Fig. 7(e). The fluorescence intensities of N-CNDs were gradually decreased with an increase in the concentration of Fe3+ ion which is clear from the inset of Fig. 7(e). As shown in Fig. 7(f), the linear plot observed for Fe3+ ion concentration (10–50 μM) dependent fluorescence intensity ((F0 – F)/F0) of N-CNDs with linear regression value (R2) of 0.994 (S/N = 5). The results suggest that the ferric ions could selectively and sensitively quench the fluorescence of N-CNDs, with a detection limit of 2.5 μM based on the Eq. (2). According to the guideline limit of Fe3+ concentration (5.36 μM) proposed by the World Health Organization (WHO), the LOD for Fe3+ detection in this study was much lower, showing that the synthesized N-CNDs are promising in detecting trace amount of Fe3+ [24]. Table 1 shows the comparison of ferric ion sensing/detection with different carbonaceous nanomaterials. In the present work, the detecting limit 2.5 μM which is auspiciously low value than the previously reported values for the sensing ferric ions [64,66–73]. Further, the fluorescence photographic images of N-CNDs in the presence of various metal ions such as Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ with the concentration of each metal ion was 1000 μM under a UV lamp with 365 nm source as shown in Fig. 8 . Visual observation of the process also shows the non-fluorescent metal adduct which might be due to Fe3+ ion bind to the carboxyl, hydroxyl and/or nitrogen functional groups.
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
S
where σ is the standard deviation of the signals (n = 5) and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot.

The synthesized N-CNDs in the presence of Fe3+ ion (after quenching of fluorescence quenching) was examined by FTIR spectroscopy analysis to determine the complex formation between functionalities of N-CNDs and Fe3+ ion and corresponding ATR-FTIR spectrum is shown as Fig. S3. The surface carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups of N-CNDs reciprocate with Fe3+ ion and produce a new electron/energy transfer which results in the fluorescence quenching of N-CNDs [30]. The ATR-FTIR spectra of N-CNDs in the presence of Fe3+ ion, the vibrational bands between 400 and 1000 cm−1 exhibit apparent changes compared to as-synthesized N-CNDs, which suggest that the surface functional groups of N-CNDs produce a new electronic effect due to the binding of Fe3+ ion. In addition, in the presence of Fe3+ ion, as compared to as-synthesized N-CNDs the absorption band intensities of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups are considerably decreased for N-CNDs. This suggests that the fluorescence decrement of N-CNDs is due to the synergism of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups with Fe3+ ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Viability and imaging of human colon cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the synthesized N-CNDs used as a fluorescent probe for imaging due to its excellent optical properties and good water solubility. The living cell was incubated with the N-CNDs, in order to check for internalization of the N-CNDs into the cells that could be analyzed by confocal microscopy for fluorescence bioimaging. Before utilizing the N-CNDs as a fluorescent probe for imaging, cytotoxicity was checked. Because biocompatibility is one of the essential properties for live-cell imaging applications. Fig. S4 depicts the cytotoxicity (cell viability) of the synthesized N-CNDs with the different concentrations of human colon cancer (HCT-116) cells. It can be seen that the cell viability decreased from 100 to 96 % when the N-CNDs concentration is increased from 0 to 200 μg mL−1, indicating that the N-CNDs does not lead to human colon cancer cell death significantly [74]. The N-CNDs did not exhibit any cytotoxicity towards cells and are tolerable even at high concentrations (200 μg mL−1), which confirm that the synthesized N-CNDs have good biocompatibility against human colon cancer cells. Hence, the N-CNDs have potentially used as a fluorescent probe for live-cell imaging. The fluorescence images of human colon cancer (HCT-116) cells in the presence and absence of N-CNDs/N-CNDs + Fe3+ under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 9 ). The human colon cancer cells did not display any fluorescence under the different excitation wavelengths in the absence of N-CNDs. The N-CNDs treated human colon cancer cells have exhibited bright red, blue, and green emissions under the excitation wavelengths of 555, 405 and 488 nm, respectively. Results revealed that the synthesized N-CNDs were easily entering and uniformly distributed into the cells which lead to image cells with bright fluorescent signals [75]. The N-CNDs treated human colon cancer cells have exhibited the multicolor fluorescence but no fluorescence was observed for N-CNDs + Fe3+ complex treated human colon cancer cells. The fluorescence intensity suddenly dropped by the addition of Fe3+, indicates that Fe3+ ions effectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of N-CNDs inside the cells. This result demonstrates that the synthesized N-CNDs fit for the detection of Fe3+ ions in the living cell and the intrinsic multicolor properties of the N-CNDs constitute a significant advantage for cell imaging applications.

In summary, we have demonstrated an economic and one-step hydrothermal method for the synthesis of tunable fluorescent N-CNDs using M. floribunda fruit (natural material) and ammonia as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. HRTEM results revealed that the synthesized N-CNDs have a relatively narrow size distribution between 3 and 5 nm with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. FTIR and XPS confirm the functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2) on the surface of N-CNDs during the synthesis process. The synthesized N-CNDs exhibited the excitation-dependent emission with a favorable QY (18 %) and excellent photostability under the UV lamp with a 365 nm source. Due to amazing optical properties, good water solubility/dispersibility, and rich hydrophilic functionalities, M. floribunda fruit juice derived N-CNDs have been successfully applied for selective fluorescence sensing of ferric ions with a detection limit of 2.5 μM. In addition, the synthesized N-CNDs was employed as ideal multicolor fluorescent probes for human colon cancer (HCT-116) cell imaging. The cell imaging results confirm the internalized and uniform distribution of synthesized N-CNDs into the whole body of cells. Furthermore, the HCT-116 cells in the presence of N-CNDs+Fe3+ adduct does not show any fluorescence suggesting that Fe3+ions are effectively sensing by the N-CNDs and resulting turn-off fluorescence inside the cells. Thus, it suggests that such M. floribunda fruit juice derived N-CNDs can be used as low-cost, eco-friendly tunable fluorescent probes for both biosensing and bioimaging. On the basis of good biocompatibility and rich functional groups on the surface of N-CNDs can be a good candidate to attach the anticancer drugs without any modification, the successfully integrated N-CNDs+ anticancer drugs would be potentially considered as a safe material for anticancer drug delivery in near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675) and the Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.112336.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
